coredata.dc:title: Carboxymethylcellulose hydrogels with stable carbon quantum dots: Enabling dynamic fluorescence modulation, automatic erasure, and secure information encryption
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Qiuyan
Liu, Hao
Li, Dongxu
Dai, Juguo
Xia, Long
Jiang, Jia
Xu, Yiting
Zeng, Birong
Luo, Weiang
Dai, Lizong
coredata.dc:description: Inspired by “disappear after reading”, a time-modulated encryption hydrogel was synthesized by carboxymethyl cellulose with carbon quantum dots. Carboxymethyl cellulose in this system stabilized carbon quantum dots, which ensured the whole hydrogel worked well. The encryption/decryption of information depended on pH adjustment, application of EDTA and Cr (VI). Furthermore, an in-depth analysis of the fluorescence change mechanism uncovered that fluorescence quenching was potentially influenced by internal filtering effects and static quenching, which involved the amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups present within the hydrogel.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carboxymethyl cellulose
Carbon quantum dots
Hydrogel
Time-modulated
Encryption-decryption-erasing

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 52273275 ), the Xiamen Public Technology Service Platform Oriented Project ( YDZX20193502000004 ), and Collaborative Innovation Center of Petrochemical Downstream Raw Materials and New Materials Industry .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of CDH hydrogel.

Characterization of CDH hydrogel. (a) SEM images; (b) FTIR spectra; (c) XPS measured spectra; (d-e) XPS spectra associated with O 1s and N 1s; (f) excitation-related PL spectra; (g) UV–Vis spectra and PL spectra. (h-i) Influence of frequency and shear strain on the storage modulus (G′), loss modulus (G″) of CDH hydrogel.
a-d) Fluorescence spectra and the CIE1931 coordinates of CDH-EDTA and CDH hydrogels immersed in Cr(VI) for different time periods.

Schematic diagram of CDH hydrogel for information storage and encryption. a) Heart-shaped encryption-decryption is achieved by ion-printing technique introducing EDTA and using its chelation with Cr(VI) to slow down the decay of fluorescence. b) Introducing EDTA and EDTA+H+ through ion printing, printing Chinese characters “人” (people) and Chinese characters “从” (from) respectively, dipping into Cr(VI), leading to the display of decrypted information Chinese characters “众” (Crowd), then decrypted information Chinese characters “从” (from), and finally disappearing.

Schematic diagram of loading information by ion printing technology, then decrypting and erasing. For example, EDTA ion-printed circular, star, pentagram, and butterfly patterns on CDH hydrogels. First, the patterns were invisible under 365 nm UV light, and then they were immersed in Cr(VI) for 15 s, which slowly appeared and then disappeared as the immersion time was prolonged.

Ion-printed CDH hydrogels immersed in Cr(VI) present encrypted information at different times, where red represents EDTA+H+ and blue represents EDTA. For example, firstly, “9” (nine), “8” (eight), “众” (Crowd) and “森” (full of trees) patterns were printed using EDTA and EDTA+H+. At first, the patterns were invisible under the 365 nm UV light, then immersed in Cr(VI) for 25 s, and with the immersion time prolonged, it appeared and then disappeared partly to finally disappeared completely.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) and excitation spectrum of CDH hydrogels. (b) Fluorescence decay curves of CDH hydrogels and CDH hydrogels with adsorbed Cr(VI). (c–d) Fluorescence decay curves of CDH hydrogels and CDH hydrogels with adsorbed Cr(VI) fitted by second-order exponential functions.

(a) FTIR analysis of EDTA-Cr, CDH, CDH-Cr, and CDH-EDTA-Cr. (b) XPS spectra of EDTA-Cr, CDH-Cr, and CDH-EDTA-Cr associated with Cr 2p. (c-d) XPS spectra of EDTA-Cr, CDH, CDH-Cr, and CDH-EDTA-Cr associated with O 1s and N 1s.

head.ce:title: Carboxymethylcellulose hydrogels with stable carbon quantum dots: Enabling dynamic fluorescence modulation, automatic erasure, and secure information encryption

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials, Xiamen Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials
Xiamen Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials, Xiamen Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials, Xiamen University, Xiamen, 361005, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Inspired by “disappear after reading”, a time-modulated encryption hydrogel was synthesized by carboxymethyl cellulose with carbon quantum dots. Carboxymethyl cellulose in this system stabilized carbon quantum dots, which ensured the whole hydrogel worked well. The encryption/decryption of information depended on pH adjustment, application of EDTA and Cr (VI). Furthermore, an in-depth analysis of the fluorescence change mechanism uncovered that fluorescence quenching was potentially influenced by internal filtering effects and static quenching, which involved the amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups present within the hydrogel.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, people's demands for the capacity, accuracy, and security of information increased (Hong et al., 2020; Ren et al., 2020). Efficiently information encryption and decryption methods have attracted much attention from researchers, such as, security codes to holographic anti-counterfeiting (Lim et al., 2019; Lu & Chi, 2003; Pfeiffer & Fischer, 2022; Wang et al., 2014), and fluorescent identification (Xu et al., 2022; Yan et al., 2023), etc. The encryption-decryption process ensured the security of information in the process of transmission (Shi et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2022). However, the decrypted information still has the risk of secondary leakage if it was improperly processed. Therefore, there was an urgent need to develop new intelligent display modes and prepare advanced information storage devices that can destroy encrypted information in time after it was decrypted to enhance the security of information.

Smart hydrogels have a color or shape change according to external stimuli such as pH value (Le et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2020), temperature (Lou et al., 2022), UV light (Li, Chen et al., 2019), etc. They have immense potential in the field of information technology, particularly for camouflage and decryption. Among them, hydrogels with fluorescence recognition were highly sought after for information protection because of their easy detection and fast response (Hai et al., 2018; Si et al., 2021). For instance, Zhang and colleagues have successfully built information-loaded 2D hydrogel sheets into 3D hydrogel information carriers of different shapes using dynamic borate ester bonds to achieve 3D information storage and encryption based on fluorescent polymer hydrogels (Zhang et al., 2019). Similarly, Zhu et al. have achieved the ability to encode multiple fluorescent patterns in the gel by using hydrogen bonding and chromophores to prepare fluorescent hydrogels and to perform double encryption by paper folding/paper cutting deformation (Zhu et al., 2021). Despite the significant advancements in multi-level encryption and controlled information deformation, the complexity, high costs, and ecological unfriendliness of the associated operational procedures have impeded further progress.

Carbon quantum dots (CD) were widely regarded as highly promising luminescent materials in terms of broad excitation spectrum, adjustable fluorescence color, and excellent photostability. Moreover, their simple synthesis method and affordable cost significantly contributed to their potential applications in various fields, including chemical sensing (Hu et al., 2015; Zheng et al., 2021), bioimaging and optoelectronic devices (Chinnathambi & Shirahata, 2019; Devi & Wangoo, 2023; Dimos, 2018; Parvin & Mandal, 2016). Furthermore, carbon quantum dots are often assembled with carriers to form fluorescent materials with good anti-counterfeiting properties (Cheng et al., 2022; Li et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2022).

Cellulose, as the most abundant environmentally friendly resource, represented a highly promising material solution for achieving both a carbon peak and carbon-neutral society. In addition, carboxymethyl cellulose(CMC) was widely used in medical (Antosik et al., 2019; Kong et al., 2020; Roy & Rhim, 2020), food and daily chemical industries due to its excellent solubility (Deshmukh et al., 2022; He et al., 2021; Orehek et al., 2014; Raval et al., 2023), film-forming, and mechanical properties. In these applications, CMC has emerged as a highly suitable carrier for carbon quantum dots, aligning with the principles of the green economy and sustainable development.

Physical mixing and chemical reactions were the main ways to connect cellulose with carbon quantum dots. The former was straightforward, but presented drawbacks including compromised structural stability, limited compatibility, and the risk of carbon quantum dot detachment, resulting in diminished fluorescence. On the contrast, one notable advantage of CMC lies in its abundant hydroxyl and carboxylic acid groups, facilitating the grafting of amino-rich carbon quantum dots. This process generated robust bonding interactions, ensured uniform dispersion within the hydrogel matrix, augmented mechanical properties, and endowed chemical stability to the carbon quantum dots, effectively averting aggregation-induced quenching. Junka et al., employed the EDC-NHS catalyst system, they synthesized a CNF nanopaper incorporating carbon quantum dots via solution impregnation for achieving tunable luminescence (Junka et al., 2014). Xue et al. employed carbon quantum dots that were grafted attached to oxidized cellulose nanofibers to fabricate sensors dedicated to the detection of Fe3+ (Xue et al., 2020). Nevertheless, knowledge about hydrogel systems incorporating carbon quantum dots grafted onto carboxymethyl cellulose was limited, particularly in the field of anti-counterfeiting applications.

We demonstrated a straightforward method for preparing a composite hydrogel, referred to as CDH, by using carbon quantum dots as the fluorescent substance and CMC as the carrier. The CDH hydrogel was synthesized by grafting carbon quantum dots onto carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC-CD) and subsequently subjecting it to a free radical polymerization reaction with acrylamide. In CDH, the presence of CMC ensured the uniform dispersion of carbon quantum dots within the hydrogel, prevented their leaching and migration outside the gel during swelling, while CMC-CD enhanced fluorescence characteristics of the CDH hydrogel. Furthermore, the fluorescence of the hydrogel could be effectively quenched by Cr(VI). Notably, EDTA slowed down the quenching of hydrogel fluorescence by Cr (VI), and pH accelerated the quenching of its fluorescence in Cr (VI). Therefore, it has caused a variance in the fluorescence of the hydrogel at the same time. Regarding this property, the hydrogel utilized ion-printing technology to achieve seamless integration of information storage, decryption, and erasure.

Sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (NaCMC, Mw = 96,558 g/mol), N-(3-dimethylaminoprop-yl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 98 %), N-hydroxysuc-cinimide (NHS, 98 %), citric acid (98 %), ethylenediamine (99.5 %), acrylamide (AM, 99 %), potassium persulfate (KPS, 99.5 %), N,N′-Methylenebis (acrylamide) (MBA, 99 %) and disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA-2Na, 98 %), were purchased from Aladdin Corporation. All chemical reagents were used directly without further purification. The device used was shown in Fig. S1.

Sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (NaCMC) was dissolved in a 0.1 M NaCl solution, achieving a concentration of 0.2 g/100 mL. Subsequently, its molecular weight was ascertained via Ubbelohde viscometry. The molecular weight of NaCMC was derived from its intrinsic viscosity, calculated employing the “Mark–Houwink” equation, as follows:
η
= K ×
M a

In this context, “K” represented the solvent constant, “a” denoted the polymer shape factor, “[η]” signified the intrinsic viscosity, and “M” standed for the molecular weight of NaCMC. As per the literature (Alvarez-Lorenzo et al., 2001), the values for these variables were defined as follows: K = 1.23 × 10^(−4) and a = 0.91.

The assessment of the degree of substitution involved several steps. Initially, 0.5 g of desiccated NaCMC underwent gentle ashing within a temperature range of 450 to 550 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, the resulting ash was dissolved in 100 mL of distilled water. From this solution, 20 mL was subjected to titration using 0.1 N sulphuric acid, with methyl red serving as the indicator. After the initial endpoint, the solution was brought to a boil and titrated again until a sharp endpoint was reached. The determination of carboxymethyl content was based on the degree of substitution and was calculated as follows:
Degree of Substitution
DS
= 0.162 × B /
1 − 0.08 × B
B = 0.1 × b / G
where, b was the volume (in mL) of 0.1 N sulphuric acid and G was the mass of pure NaCMC in grams.

Carbon quantum dots were synthesized by a hydrothermal method, and the synthesis route was shown in Scheme S1. First, citric acid (4.2 g) was dissolved in deionized water (100 mL). Then ethylenediamine (5.36 mL) was added, and the resulting solution was transferred to a PTFE autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 5 h. After the autoclave reactor was cooled naturally to room temperature, the product was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1000 KDa) for 24 h to obtain water-soluble carbon quantum dot (0.1 wt%). Characterization of carbon quantum dots were shown in Figs. S2 and S3.

The carbon quantum dots were grafted onto sodium carboxymethyl cellulose via a condensation reaction (Wu et al., 2019) and the synthesis route was shown in Scheme S2. Initially, NaCMC (2 wt%) was dissolved in a DMSO: H2O = 3:2 solution during stirring. Then, an acetic acid buffer (0.5 mL), EDC (6 mg), NHS (23 mg) and CD (1000 μL) were added and stirred for 2 h at 25 °C. Subsequently, CMC-CD was obtained.

CDH hydrogels were prepared using free radical polymerization reaction (Seven & Sahiner, 2013). Initially, CMC-CD suspensions with different carbon quantum dot contents (0, 50, 100, 600, 800, 1000, 1200, 1400, 1600, 1800 μL of 0.1 wt% CD aqueous solution) were mixed with AM (1.5 g) and stirred for 1 h. Subsequently, the cross-linker MBA (20 mg) and KPS (10 mg) were added and sonicated for 2 min. Then the mixture was transferred to a mold and reacted at 60 °C for 2 h to form CDH hydrogels. The final prepared fluorescent hydrogels were named as CDH-1, CDH-2, CDH-3, CDH-4, CDH-5, CDH-6, CDH-7, CDH-8, CDH-9, and CDH-10.

The analysis of the prepared samples encompassed various techniques. Surface morphology was observed using a SU-70 scanning electron microscope (SEM, Sigma 300, Japan). The chemical structure was characterized through Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet iS10, USA) and analyzed using the attenuated total reflection (ATR) method. Microstructures were examined via transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos F200s, USA). Prior to TEM analysis, samples were ultrasonically dispersed in an ethanol solution, and a uniform drop was deposited onto a copper grid. UV–visible spectra were acquired with a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV2550, Shimadzu, Japan) in the wavelength range of 200–680 nm. Crystal structure analysis was conducted using X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker-axs) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). The XRD measurements were performed at a current and voltage of 40 mA and 40 kV, respectively, with scanning angles ranging from 5° to 90°. Fluorescence emission spectra (PL) were acquired using an FLS9800 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) with a 5 nm slit width, 400 V voltage, and a scanning range of 300–650 nm. Thermal stability analysis was performed using a Thermogravimetric Analyzer (Model TGA5500) with a dynamic temperature program spanning 25–600 °C and a heating rate of 10 °C/min The chemical composition and elemental content were determined using an X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (XPS, VG Scientific, UK). Fluorescence lifetimes were measured using an Edinburgh FLS980 transient fluorescence spectrometer with laser excitation at 375 nm. Modulus testing (oscillatory mode) was conducted on an Anton Paar MCR302 rheometer. Hydrogel slices, 20 mm in diameter and 2 mm thick, were prepared and subjected to 0.5 % strain, frequencies ranging from 0.1 to 100 Hz, and a constant temperature of 25 °C. Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectra, including 1H and 13C NMR, were recorded on a Bruker Advanced II AV400MHz NMR spectrometer using DMSO as the solvent.

CDH hydrogel synthesis process was shown in Fig. 1 . A series of CDH hydrogels with different carbon quantum dot contents were obtained by grafting carbon quantum dots onto the main chain of sodium carboxymethyl cellulose with molecular weights of 96,558 g/mol [degree of substitution (DS): 0.43] (Table S1, Fig. S4), then added acrylamide monomer, and performed free radical polymerization reaction in the presence of cross-linker MBA and initiator KPS. The fluorescence intensity of CDH hydrogels increased firstly and then decreased with the carbon quantum dot content increased, as excess carbon quantum dots caused aggregation leading to weakened fluorescence (Jiang et al., 2018). Among them, CDH-6 had the strongest fluorescence intensity with an absolute quantum yield of 41 % (Fig. S5). Therefore, CDH-6 was chosen for all subsequent studies.

The 3D network structure and morphology of the CDH hydrogels was shown in Fig. 2a. To assess the morphology of CDH hydrogels, SEM was performed on both CMC and CMC-CD (Fig. S6). The CMC-CD exhibited a reduced pore size, indicated that the interaction between carbon quantum dots and carboxymethylcellulose enhanced the cross-linking density of CDH hydrogels (Lv et al., 2022). The FTIR spectra of NaCMC, CMC-CD, AM and CDH were shown in Fig. 2b. NaCMC showed characteristic peaks at 3320 cm−1 and 1587 cm−1 attributed to the -OH stretching vibration and CO stretching vibration (Bhattacharyya & Ray, 2013). The CMC-CD spectrum displayed a novel peak at 1654 cm−1 (Wu et al., 2019) ascribed to the stretching vibration of the CO bond within the O=C-NH moiety, signifying the successful grafting of the carbon quantum dot onto the NaCMC matrix. For AM, the absorption peak at 3165 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration peak of -NH2 on the amide group (-CONH2) in AM (Li et al., 2022). Moreover, the absence of the peak at 1060 cm−1 in the CDH hydrogel spectrum indicated that CMC-CD underwent radical polymerization, resulting in the formation of CDH hydrogel (Luo et al., 2021). To further corroborate the grafting of carbon quantum dots onto carboxymethylcellulose, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses of CD and CMC-CD were conducted. The high-resolution N (1s) spectra for CD and CMC-CD were depicted in Fig. S7b, e, where the nitrogen peaks of CD manifested at 400.3 eV (NH) and 399.0 eV (NC). In contrast, the CMC-CD peak exhibited a blue shift, indicated an interaction between CD and CMC (Zou et al., 2020). The high-resolution O (1s) spectra of CD were deconvolved into two binding peaks at 531.8 and 530.9 eV, corresponding to CO and CO groups, respectively (Fig. S7c). The shifting of these peaks in CMC-CD spectra signified functionalization by the CO group (Lv et al., 2022), culminating in the formation of an amide bond and successful grafting with CMC. In addition, it was observed that sodium carboxymethyl cellulose effectively stabilizes carbon quantum dots (Fig. S8). After a storage period of 7 days, the fluorescence intensity of CD decreased significantly compared with CMC-CD.

To further understand the chemical structure of CDH hydrogels, XPS was applied, as shown in Fig. 2c, the three bands seen in CDH hydrogels at 531, 399, and 285 eV were associated with O 1s, N 1s, and C 1s, respectively. Fig. 2d showed the high-resolution O 1s spectrum divided into two components of CO (531.1 eV) and -C-O (532.0 eV). In the N1s region (Fig. 2e), two distinct peaks are observed at 399.4 eV and 400.5 eV, corresponding to the NH group and the N+ group, respectively (Fei et al., 2022). To investigate the fluorescence properties of the carbon quantum dots, the CDH hydrogels were tested for fluorescence and UV spectra (Fig. 2f). The emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths 333, 343, 353, 363, 373, and 383 nm were not shifted, indicating the hydrogels showed no excitation wavelength dependence (Siddique et al., 2021). The UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of the CDH hydrogels were shown in Fig. 2g. The absorption spectrum had a peak at 340 nm, which came from the carbon quantum dot (Zhu et al., 2013).The optimal excitation wavelength was 363 nm and the optimal emission wavelength was 437 nm. To gain further insights into the rheological properties of CDH hydrogels, we conducted frequency scanning tests. As depicted in Fig. 2h, the energy storage modulus (G′) was much higher than the loss modulus (G″) over the entire frequency range, and their curves displayed a parallel upward slow inclination. This result suggested that the limited frequency dependence of CDH hydrogels, implying a small presence of cross-linking relaxation. It was possible due to the chemical cross-linking between carboxymethyl cellulose and acrylamide, which increased the entanglement of the polymer chains in the CDH hydrogel network (Kumar et al., 2018), resulting in the hydrogels exhibiting good elastic behavior. Besides, dynamic strain scanning tests were performed, and the outcomes are illustrated in Fig. 2i. Notably, for strain levels below 0.39 %, there was no discernible correlation between strain variations and the rheological properties of the CDH hydrogel. When the strain was in the region between 0.39 % and 454.4 %, G′ > G″, which was elastic deformation. When the strain exceeded 454.4 %, G″ > G′, indicated CDH hydrogel rupture.

As expected, carbon quantum dots endowed the CDH hydrogels showed an intense blue fluorescence. Interestingly, dropped EDTA into the CDH hydrogel and then immersed it in Cr(VI) solution revealed that the fluorescence intensity diminished with increased immersion time until the quenching (Fig. 3a). Corresponding to the change in CIE coordinates from (0.15, 0.078) to (0.15, 0.098), it was found that the color did not shift, but only the intensity changed (Fig. 3b). The fluorescence intensity of the CDH hydrogel was diminished a little faster compared with CDH-EDTA when it was directly immersed in Cr(VI) (Fig. 3c). This corresponds to a change in CIE coordinates from (0.15, 0.079) to (0.15, 0.10), as shown in Fig. 3d. In addition, the acid effect experiment was shown in Fig. S9. From the results can found that acid led to a rapid quenching effect. Since EDTA can coordinated with many other metal ions, part of the CDH hydrogel was printed with EDTA and then immersed in other metal ions, as shown in Fig. S10, and it was found that only the Cr(VI) could make the CDH hydrogel fluorescence quenching rapidly, thus creating a time difference with the area printed with EDTA. As CDH contains biopolymers, thermal stability data were important. Here we also performed thermogravimetric tests on CDH, CDH-EDTA and CDH-EDTA+H+ (Fig. S11). The initial phase, occurred between room temperature and 255 °C, was attributed to dehydration processes, leading to a 14.25 % reduction in the hydrogel's weight. The subsequent phase, observed from 255 °C to 343 °C, resulted in a weight loss of approximately 49.3 %, signifying the decomposition of crosslinked polymer chains. Notably, in this latter phase, CDH-EDTA experienced a more significant weight loss compared to the pure CDH hydrogel. This phenomenon can be ascribed to the oxidation of carboxyl groups in EDTA, leading to the production of CO2 at higher temperatures (He et al., 2020). The thermogravimetric curves of CDH-EDTA+H+ and CDH-EDTA exhibited similar trends. However, the mass loss of CDH-EDTA+H+ was lower than of CDH-EDTA, which could be attributed to the presence of the acid resulting in a more tightly cross-linked polymer.

Prior research displayed the different fluorescence quenching rate of EDTA and Cr(VI) as well as acid acting on the hydrogels. The subsequent fluorescence experiment were based on above mentioned nature. Our approach, illustrated in Fig. 4a, the CDH hydrogel was converted into a CDH-EDTA hydrogel locally by introducing the metal complexing agent EDTA using ion printing technique. The fluorescence of CDH hydrogel was further attenuated through the addition of Cr(VI), which also chelated with EDTA and slowed down the reduction in CDH-EDTA hydrogel fluorescence. This process resulted in a distinction between areas with and without printed EDTA, forming a heart shape in the middle of the CDH hydrogel. Subsequently, the heart shape disappeared as the immersion time in Cr(VI) was extended. Based on the pH-dependent fluorescence of CDH hydrogels, we studied CDH-EDTA+H+ and CDH-EDTA hydrogels. The interaction of H+ ions with Cr(VI) rapidly diminished the fluorescence of CDH-EDTA+H+ hydrogels, whereas the interaction of CDH-EDTA hydrogels with Cr(VI) caused the fluorescence to diminish slowly, resulting in a difference in fluorescence intensity over time. For instance, in Fig. 4b, there was a Chinese character “众” (Crowd), which was a combination of the characters for “人” (people) and “从” (from). Initially, the character “人” (people) was printed using EDTA, and then the character “从” (from) was printed using EDTA+H+. Upon immersion in Cr(VI) over time, the fluorescence of the “人” (people) slowly quenched and the character “众” (Crowd) transformed into “从” (from), eventually disappearing completely. This process can inadvertently generated misinformation, leading to confusion. However, it can enhanced material security and inspire innovative approaches to information encryption.

Patterns like circles, hearts, pentagrams, and butterflies can be imprinted onto CDH hydrogels using EDTA. Initially invisible due to consistent fluorescence intensity before and after treatment. Simultaneously, employing the time-based fluorescence attenuation caused by Cr(VI), the concealed designs slowly became visible and then gradually fade away, as depicted in Fig. 5 . The study delved into the optical responses of CDH hydrogels to Cr(VI) exposure and its impact on fluorescence within specific shapes. Results indicated that heart-shaped regions remained unseen until exposed to Cr(VI). Upon immersion in Cr(VI), the heart-shaped region imprinted with EDTA became visible. However, with prolonged Cr(VI) exposure, the heart-shaped region vanished due to the limited concentration of EDTA, resulting from limiting the Cr(VI) and carbon quantum dot interactions only for a limited duration of time. Similarly, other shapes like circles, pentagrams, and butterflies followed a similar pattern. Initially hidden, becoming visible upon exposure, and eventually self-erasing. This mechanism involved encryption, decryption, and self-erasure, allowed information to vanish after reading and aided in preventing unauthorized access.

We investigated the influence of different pH levels of Cr(VI) on CD and CMC-CD (Fig. S13). The pH levels had a negligible effect on both CD and CMC-CD, likely due to their surface characteristics and the presence of Cr(VI) (Dong et al., 2012). Upon further investigation into the effect of various pH values of Cr(VI) on CDH hydrogels, illustrated in Fig. S12, we revealed a pronounced pH sensitivity in CDH hydrogels. As the pH decreased, the fluorescence intensity of CDH hydrogels diminished, and at pH = 2, the fluorescence was effectively quenched. CDH, CDH-EDTA, and CDH-EDTA+H+ hydrogels were immersed in a Cr(VI) solution. Over time, the fluorescence intensity of CDH-EDTA+H+ decreased rapidly compared with that of CDH hydrogel, while the decrease in fluorescence intensity of CDH-EDTA exhibited a delay (Fig. S14). The factors influencing the fluorescence intensity of CDH hydrogel were complex. To understand the impact of individual factors, we conducted separate studies on the effects of pH, EDTA, and Cr(VI) (Fig. S15). The results indicated that there was little impact on CDH's fluorescence intensity with increasing EDTA concentration. However, pH and Cr(VI) continued to affect the fluorescence of CDH hydrogels. At pH = 2, the effect became most pronounced with higher Cr(VI) concentrations. Numbers “9” (nine) and “8” (eight), as well as the Chinese characters “众” (Crowd) and “森” (full of trees), were printed on CDH hydrogels using solutions containing the corresponding ions (EDTA+H+ for red and EDTA for blue) (Fig. 6 ). To illustrate, the number “9” (nine) was visible initially because of the contrast between the fluorescence of the unprinted and printed areas. Subsequently, the number “9” (nine) became “3” (three) and was visible as the fluorescence of the printed EDTA region decreased with prolonged immersion in Cr(VI). However, the number “3” (three) vanishes as the Cr(VI) immersion time was increased further. Likewise, messages “6” (six), “从” (from), and “林” (forest) appeared following the display of the messages “8” (eight), “众” (Crowd), and “森” (full of trees). These symbols cannot be identified without prolonged immersion in Cr(VI), adding an additional layer of security to the encryption of the information.

To elucidate the mechanism of Cr(VI) quenching fluorescence of CDH hydrogels, the optical properties of Cr(VI) and CDH hydrogels were investigated (Fig. 7a), and it was found that the UV–vis spectral band of Cr(VI) overlapped with the fluorescence excitation spectrum of CDH hydrogels, indicating that Cr(VI) had an internal filtering effect on CDH hydrogels (Duan et al., 2020). Due to the absorption of Cr(VI), fewer photons reached the CDH hydrogel, led to the fluorescence quenching. In addition, the fluorescence decay curves of CDH hydrogels were obtained before and after dropping Cr(VI) (Fig. 7b-d), and the corresponding average values were calculated to be 7.32 and 7.15 ns, respectively. The similar lifetimes indicated a static quenching effect, possibly attributed to the formation of non-fluorescent matrix complexes between functional groups on the surface of the carbon quantum dots and Cr(VI) (Huang et al., 2022).

Discrepancies in fluorescence changes in CDH hydrogels induced by Cr(VI) and EDTA were closely examined using FTIR and XPS spectra. The FTIR spectrum of the current CDH hydrogel (Fig. 8a) exhibited a distinct absorption peak corresponding to OH stretching vibrations at 3320 cm−1. Additionally, the bands at 1650 cm−1 and 1406 cm−1 correspond to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching, respectively, of the COO−groups (Zhang et al., 2021). The hydrogel (CDH-Cr) after immersion in Cr(VI) showed a new band at 940 cm−1 belonging to Cr(VI) (Xi et al., 2022). Moreover, the intensity of the peak vibration at 3320 cm−1, 1650 cm−1 and 1406 cm−1 was significantly lower for CDH-EDTA-Cr compared to the peak for CDH-Cr. The above changes confirmed that the amino, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups of CDH hydrogels played a role in the difference of fluorescence changes (Fei et al., 2022). The addition of hydrogen ions to EDTA resulted in protonation and positive charge of the amino group on the CDH hydrogel, which was printed with the EDTA+H+ region. Simultaneously, Cr(VI) carried a negative charge upon contact with hydrogen ions. This electrostatic attraction between the negatively charged Cr(VI) and the positively charged CDH hydrogel could facilitated quenching (Mei et al., 2019). To further investigated the mechanism of action, we performed XPS analysis. Fig. 8b showed the high-resolution Cr 2p spectra with Cr 2p1/2 and Cr 2p3/2 peaks located at 588.7, 586.2 eV and 579.5, 576.7 eV, respectively, indicating that a reduction reaction occurred and part of Cr(VI) was converted to Cr(III) (Miretzky & Fernandez Cirelli, 2010). And the peaks of CDH-EDTA-Cr sample were shifted, indicating that EDTA may be involved in the reduction of Cr(VI) (Li, Yan, et al., 2019). Fig. 8c showed the high-resolution XPS results of O 1s. 531.1 eV and 532.0 eV appeared on the CDH hydrogel mainly attributed to CO, CO, while both of the above peaks were shifted in the oxygen spectrum of CDH-Cr, and this change indicated a strong binding ability between carboxyl and hydroxyl groups and Cr(III). The oxygen spectrum of CDH-EDTA-Cr, both shifted to a higher level, indicating that EDTA had this binding energy, and different from the oxygen spectrum of EDTA-Cr, indicating the involvement of other substances. According to the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 8d), the CDH hydrogel immersed in Cr(VI) exhibited a shift in peak position from 400.5 to 400.1 and an intensity enhancement. This change was likely attributed to the formation of a complex between N and Cr (Fei et al., 2022). The nitrogen spectrum of CDH-EDTA-Cr shifted from 400.1 to 400.3, and was different from that of EDTA-Cr, indicating that some of the carbon quantum dots complexed with Cr(VI) as well.

A robust biomass hydrogel with carbon quantum dots and CMC were successfully synthesized in this work for information encryption. The disadvantage of easy aggregation of carbon quantum dots in hydrogels was improved by CMC. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of CDH hydrogel will be affected by Cr(VI), EDTA, and pH. Utilizing ion-printing technology can achieve different sets of information encoding. Among them, Cr(VI) suppressed the fluorescence of CDH hydrogels, EDTA slowed down the rate of fluorescence quenching, while pH = 2 rapidly diminished their fluorescence, resulting in distinct fluorescence patterns that allowed for information visibility. With prolonged immersion of the CDH hydrogel in Cr(VI), the fluorescence was entirely extinguished, effectively eliminating the embedded information. In conclusion, we have successfully developed an affordable, environmentally friendly, easy-to-use CDH hydrogel with a robust encryption-decryption-checking mechanism, thus providing a new and innovative method for information encryption.

Qiuyan Luo, proposed the idea and designed the experiment, synthesized the materials and analyzed the data and completed the original manuscript. Hao Liu helped in SEM imaging, Dongxu Li and Juguo Dai performed fluorescence lifetime analysis. Long Xia and Jia Jiang helped in chemical synthesis. Birong Zeng and Weiang Luo provided test suggestions. Yiting Xu and Lizong Dai supervised research and reviewed the paper. All the authors discussed the results and reviewed the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52273275), the Xiamen Public Technology Service Platform Oriented Project (YDZX20193502000004), and Collaborative Innovation Center of Petrochemical Downstream Raw Materials and New Materials Industry.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2023.121610.
